content:
A bulb is emitted electromagnetic radiation of $$660 \mathrm{~nm}$$ wavelength. The total energy of radiation is $$3 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~J}$$. The number of emitted photon will be

$$\left(h=6.6 \times 10^{-34}\right) \mathrm{J} \times \mathrm{s}, c=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$
explanation:
Let, number of photons $$=n$$

$$\therefore \quad E=n \cdot h v=n \cdot \frac{h c}{\lambda}$$

where, $$v=$$ frequency, $$c=$$ velocity of light, $$\lambda=$$ wavelength

$$\therefore \quad n=\frac{E \times \lambda}{h c}=\frac{3 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~J} \times 660 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js} \times 3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}}=10$$

$$n=10$$

So, the number of emitted photon $$=10$$

Hence, option (b) is the correct.

content:
Assertion : The chemical properties of different isotope are same.

Reason : Isotopes having same number of neutron.

options.content: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true, but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true, but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.

explanation:
Assertion : The chemical properties of different isotopes are the same.

Reason : Isotopes have the same number of neutrons.

Option C

If assertion is true, but reason is false.

The chemical properties of different isotopes are indeed the same because chemical properties are determined by the number of electrons and the arrangement of electrons in an atom, which is defined by the atomic number (number of protons). Isotopes of an element have the same atomic number but different numbers of neutrons. Therefore, they exhibit the same chemical behavior.

However, the reason given is incorrect because isotopes of an element do not have the same number of neutrons. Instead, isotopes are variants of a particular chemical element that have the same number of protons but a different number of neutrons.

content: The work function of a metal is $$4.2 \mathrm{~eV}$$. If radiation of 2000 $$\mathop A\limits^o$$ fall on the metal then the kinetic energy of the fastest photoelectron is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } E_0=4.2 \mathrm{~eV} \\ & =4.2 \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \\ & =6.72 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \\ & \because \quad E=h \nu=\frac{h c}{\lambda} \\ & \therefore \quad E=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{2000 \times 10^{-10}} \\ & =9.94 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Kinetic energy of electron emitted

$$\begin{aligned} & =(9.94-6.72) \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \\ & =3.22 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$
